# Host-pathogen interactions: the attributes of virulence

### Affiliation

# Host-pathogen interactions: the attributes of virulence

### Authors

### Affiliation

## Abstract

Virulence is one of a number of possible outcomes of host-microbe interaction. As such, microbial virulence is dependent on host factors, as exemplified by the pathogenicity of avirulent microbes in immunocompromised hosts and the lack of pathogenicity of virulent pathogens in immune hosts. Pathogen-centered views of virulence assert that pathogens are distinguished from nonpathogens by their expression of virulence factors. Although this concept appears to apply to certain microbes that cause disease in normal hosts, it does not apply to most microbes that cause disease primarily in immunocompromised hosts. The study of virulence is fraught with the paradox that virulence, despite being a microbial characteristic, can only be expressed in a susceptible host. Thus, the question "What is a pathogen?" begs the question, "What is the outcome of the host-microbe interaction?" We propose that host damage provides a common denominator that translates into the different outcomes of host-microbe interaction.

Review

_Bacillus cereus_: Epidemiology, Virulence Factors, and Host–Pathogen Interactions
==========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Daniel Enosi Tuipulotu 1, Anukriti Mathur 1, Chinh Ngo 1, Si Ming Man 1

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tim.2020.09.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tim.2020.09.003 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0966842X20302377&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   [Bacillus cereus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/bacillus-cereus "Learn more about Bacillus cereus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an important [human pathogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/human-pathogen "Learn more about human pathogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and new findings have expanded our understanding of how this bacterium causes disease. 
*   _B. cereus_ Hemolysin BL (HBL) and nonhemolytic [enterotoxin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/enterotoxin "Learn more about enterotoxin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NHE) induce membrane pore formation, leading to activation of the [NLRP3 inflammasome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/nlrp3-inflammasome "Learn more about NLRP3 inflammasome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), systemic inflammation, and death. 
*   Lipopolysaccharide-induced tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-α factor (LITAF) and cell death-inducing P53 target 1 (CDIP1) are bona fide mammalian [surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/surface-property "Learn more about surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") receptors of HBL. These newly identified toxin receptors and the NLRP3 [inflammasome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/inflammasome "Learn more about inflammasome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") represent unique targets for potential future therapies against severe _B. cereus_ infections. 

The toxin-producing bacterium [Bacillus cereus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/bacillus-cereus "Learn more about Bacillus cereus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an important and neglected [human pathogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/human-pathogen "Learn more about human pathogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and a common cause of food poisoning. Several toxins have been implicated in disease, including the pore-forming toxins [hemolysin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/hemolysin "Learn more about hemolysin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") BL (HBL) and nonhemolytic [enterotoxin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/enterotoxin "Learn more about enterotoxin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NHE). Recent work revealed that HBL binds to the mammalian [surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/surface-property "Learn more about surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") receptors LITAF and CDIP1 and that both HBL and NHE induce [potassium efflux](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/potassium-transport "Learn more about potassium efflux from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and activate the NLRP3 [inflammasome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/inflammasome "Learn more about inflammasome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), leading to [pyroptosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pyroptosis "Learn more about pyroptosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These mammalian receptors, in part, contribute to inflammation and pathology. Other putative [virulence factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/virulence-factor "Learn more about virulence factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of _B. cereus_ include [cytotoxin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cytotoxin "Learn more about cytotoxin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") K, [cereulide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cereulide "Learn more about cereulide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), metalloproteases, [sphingomyelinase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/sphingomyelin-phosphodiesterase "Learn more about sphingomyelinase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [phospholipases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/phospholipase "Learn more about phospholipases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In this review, we highlight the latest progress in our understanding of _B. cereus_ biology, epidemiology, and pathogenesis, and discuss potential new directions for research in this field.

Section snippets
----------------

An Overview of _Bacillus cereus_
--------------------------------

_B. cereus sensu stricto_ (herein referred to as _B. cereus_) is an important cause of food poisoning in humans. Isolated in 1887, _B. cereus_ was considered a harmless contaminant for almost 80 years before it was widely accepted as a pathogen [1,2]. Since the 1960s, _B. cereus_ has gained notoriety as the etiological agent for a variety of intestinal and extraintestinal diseases [3]. These clinical manifestations include gastroenteritis, vomiting, **endophthalmitis** (see Glossary), respiratory tract

General Microbiology of _B. cereus_
-----------------------------------

_B. cereus_ is a Gram-positive, rod-shaped, and spore-forming **facultative anaerobe** [11] (Figure 2) . _B. cereus_ strains have **peritrichous flagella** [13] (Figure 2), which are involved in locomotion and/or toxin secretion [13,14]. Further, some strains are encased within a crystalline surface glycoprotein layer (**S-layer**) which covers the cell wall [15,16] (Figure 3). Proteins within the S-layer mediate the adhesion of _B. cereus_ to host cells and provide resistance to γ-ray radiation [17].

_B. cereus_

Epidemiological Landscape of _B. cereus_
----------------------------------------

Although _B. cereus_ infections have been documented worldwide, the body of literature reporting these infections mainly relates to foodborne outbreaks of gastroenteritis in isolated countries [4,17,31]. The limited global epidemiological data on _B. cereus_ infections is attributed to: (i) the mild and short-duration of symptoms and self-limiting nature of most _B. cereus_ infections, which means that individuals generally do not seek medical attention; (ii) lack of laboratory testing to confirm

Virulence Factors of _B. cereus_
--------------------------------

_B. cereus_ produces a collection of virulence factors, including pore-forming toxins, **cereulide**, **hemolysins**, enterotoxins, proteases, and **phospholipases** (Figure 3, Key Figure). These virulence factors are discussed in detail below:

Membrane-Bound Receptors of HBL in Mammalian Cells
--------------------------------------------------

HBL can form pores in the plasma membrane and exert cytotoxicity on mammalian immune and non-immune cells. Whether HBL binds to a specific surface receptor to mediate pore formation was unknown until recently. In most cases, HBL subunits bind in the order B, L 1, and L 2 [38,39,41]. A previous study has shown that the B subunit lacking its hydrophobic transmembrane region exhibited stronger binding to the cell membrane of primary mouse macrophages than the wild-type B subunit, suggesting that an

Novel Membrane-Bound Receptor(s) of NHE
---------------------------------------

Identification of LITAF and CDIP1 opens up exciting opportunities to search for other surface receptors of _B. cereus_ toxins which are important for pathogenesis. Indeed, isolates of _B. cereus_ lacking HBL can still cause disease in humans [13,93,94]. These strains probably express NHE and other virulence factors capable of contributing to the infection [13,93,94]. In the context of NHE, the three NHE subunits bind, in most cases, in the order C, B, and A. Several studies have shown that the C

Immune Response to _B. cereus_ Infection
----------------------------------------

Innate immunity mounts the first line of defense against infectious diseases. The innate immune system recognizes pathogens and danger signals via germline-encoded **pattern-recognition receptors** (**PRRs**). These PRRs include, but are not limited to, cytosolic **inflammasome** sensor proteins and surface and endosomal TLRs [95,96]. PRRs are expressed in immune cells and non-immune cells, including macrophages, dendritic cells, neutrophils, epithelial cells, endothelial cells, and natural killer (NK)

Inflammasome Responses
----------------------

The inflammasome is a cytosolic protein complex comprising a sensor protein, the adaptor protein called apoptosis-associatedspeck-like protein containing a caspase recruitment and activation domain (also known as ASC or PYCARD), and the cysteine protease caspase-1 [101,102]. The inflammasome sensor proteins belong to either the nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain-like receptors, absent in melanoma 2 (AIM2)-like receptors, or the tripartite motif family [103,104]. To date, NLRP1, **NLRP3**,

TLRs
----

The innate immune response triggered by TLRs has been predominantly characterized in mouse and rabbit models of endophthalmitis, a severe intraocular infection mostly associated with post-traumatic injury and vision loss [109]. Earlier studies have shown that TLR2 mediated **polymorphonuclear leukocytes** (**PMNs**) infiltration of the eyes in response to _B. cereus_-induced endophthalmitis in mice [110]. The lack of TLR2 did not affect intraocular growth of _B. cereus_ but reduced the secretion of

Concluding Remarks
------------------

Since the discovery of _B. cereus_, microbiological investigations have largely focused on the biology of HBL, NHE, and cereulide, leaving the role of many other toxins and enzymes undefined. In light of this knowledge gap, key questions concerning the molecular mechanisms in which _B. cereus_ virulence factors lead to disease have remained unanswered (see Outstanding Questions).

Several mammalian receptors of _B. cereus_ have now been identified. Identification of NLRP3 as a mammalian cytosolic

Glossary
--------

**5-HT3 receptor**a receptor within the ligand-gated ion channel superfamily.

**Anthrax**a potentially deadly disease which results in inflammation of the skin, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract.

**Apoptosis**a programmed cell death pathway induced by apoptotic caspases.

**Bacteremia**bacterial infection of the bloodstream.

**Blood–brain barrier**a complex network of cells within the brain’s microvasculature protecting the central nervous system against circulating toxins or pathogens.

**Cereulide**an emetic toxin produced

*   ### [Bacillus cereus sensu lato biofilm formation and its ecological importance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590207522000041)

2022, Biofilm  Show abstract Biofilm formation is a ubiquitous process of bacterial communities that enables them to survive and persist in various environmental niches. The _Bacillus cereus_ group includes phenotypically diversified species that are widely distributed in the environment. Often, _B. cereus_ is considered a soil inhabitant, but it is also commonly isolated from plant roots, nematodes, and food products. Biofilms differ in their architecture and developmental processes, reflecting adaptations to specific niches. Importantly, some _B. cereus_ strains are foodborne pathogens responsible for two types of gastrointestinal diseases, diarrhea and emesis, caused by distinct toxins. Thus, the persistency of biofilms is of particular concern for the food industry, and understanding the underlying mechanisms of biofilm formation contributes to cleaning procedures. This review focuses on the genetic background underpinning the regulation of biofilm development, as well as the matrix components associated with biofilms. We also reflect on the correlation between biofilm formation and the development of highly resistant spores. Finally, advances in our understanding of the ecological importance and evolution of biofilm formation in the _B. cereus_ group are discussed.    
*   ### [Antibacterial and physical effects of cationic starch nanofibers containing carvacrol@casein nanoparticles against Bacillus cereus in soy products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168160522000010)

2022, International Journal of Food Microbiology  Show abstract _Bacillus cereus_ (_B. cereus_) is a recognized foodborne pathogen widely distributed in various protein-rich foods, which is a huge challenge to food safety. Herein, a novel enzyme-responsive nanomaterial based on cationic starch (CSt) nanofibers loaded with carvacrol@casein nanoparticles (CL@CSNPs) was constructed (CL@CS/CSt nanofiber) to prevent the contamination of _B. cereus_ in soybean products. Considering the excellent antibacterial activity of carvacrol (CL) against _B. cereus_, CL@CSNPs were prepared by electrostatic adsorption and hydrophobic interaction and characterized by SEM and FTIR.CL@CS/CSt nanofibers with better performance were determined by comparing the physical properties of the electrospinning solution and the prepared nanofiber. Nanofibers were prepared by electrospinning technology and analyzed by SEM and AFM to investigate the size and structural morphology of fibers. FTIR analyses were done to confirm the successful embedding of CL@CSNPs in CSt nanofibers. Subsequently, the controlled release of CL was verified by GC–MS and disc diffusion method. The application experiment results indicated that the treatment based on CL@CS/CSt nanofibers reduced the _B. cereus_ in soy products by 2 log CFU/g, which reflected a significant antibacterial activity. In addition, CL@CS/CSt nanofibers could also prevent texture and chroma changes under refrigeration and maintain the sensory quality of soy products. Thus, CL@CS/CSt nanofibers appear to have great potential in controlling the contamination of soybean products by _B. cereus_ while maintaining the physical quality.

# Bacillus cereus: Epidemiology, Virulence Factors, and Host-Pathogen Interactions

### Affiliations

# Bacillus cereus: Epidemiology, Virulence Factors, and Host-Pathogen Interactions

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

The toxin-producing bacterium Bacillus cereus is an important and neglected human pathogen and a common cause of food poisoning. Several toxins have been implicated in disease, including the pore-forming toxins hemolysin BL (HBL) and nonhemolytic enterotoxin (NHE). Recent work revealed that HBL binds to the mammalian surface receptors LITAF and CDIP1 and that both HBL and NHE induce potassium efflux and activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, leading to pyroptosis. These mammalian receptors, in part, contribute to inflammation and pathology. Other putative virulence factors of B. cereus include cytotoxin K, cereulide, metalloproteases, sphingomyelinase, and phospholipases. In this review, we highlight the latest progress in our understanding of B. cereus biology, epidemiology, and pathogenesis, and discuss potential new directions for research in this field.

**Keywords:**
IL-1; IL-18; S-layer; Toll-like receptors; anthrax; bacteremia; caspase-1; cytokines; emesis; endophthalmitis; endospores; flagella; gasdermin; gasgangrene; gastroenteritis; gut microbiome; gut microbiota; mesophilic; psychrotrophic; respiratory tract infections.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Key virulence factors of Bacillus cereus include hemolysin BL, nonhemolytic enterotoxin, and cereulide, which contribute to its pathogenicity through cytotoxic effects and inflammation.
Bacillus cereus employs molecular mimicry by binding to mammalian receptors like LITAF and CDIP1, enabling it to evade immune detection and promote inflammation.
Pathogens like Bacillus cereus manipulate host responses by activating the NLRP3 inflammasome, ultimately leading to cell death and enhancing their survival and virulence.
